title.en: Client Not Paying Your Invoice? Business Dues Recovery — Notice, MSME Route and Summary Suit

answerBox.en: For unpaid business invoices: written follow-ups with a deadline, then a ₹999 advocate's notice with interest, then court — a summary suit under Order 37 CPC on your written trail. If you hold Udyam (MSME) registration, the strongest lever applies: buyers must pay within 45 days, delayed payments carry compound interest at 3× the RBI bank rate, and MSME Samadhaan facilitation councils enforce it. Limitation: 3 years per invoice.

en.title: Tighten the paper first
en.detail: Assemble per invoice: the **PO/work order or contract**, proof of delivery/completion (signed challans, email confirmations, deployed work), the invoice, and any **acknowledgement of the debt** — a "we'll clear it next month" email resets the 3-year limitation (Section 18, Limitation Act) and doubles as an admission. GST filings showing the buyer claimed input credit on your invoice are powerful corroboration.

en.title: Demand, then the ₹999 advocate notice
en.summary: Most B2B dues settle at the notice with interest claimed.
en.detail: Send a final written demand with a 7–15 day deadline. Then the **advocate's notice**: itemised invoices, contractual/late-payment interest (or 18% commercial claim), and the stated next step — summary suit or MSME reference. Finance teams route advocate notices to legal review, which is where realistic settlement offers originate.

en.title: The MSME Samadhaan route — if you're registered
en.summary: Statutory 45-day payment + 3× compound interest + council enforcement.
en.detail: Udyam-registered micro/small suppliers get the **MSMED Act** override: payment due within **45 days** of acceptance regardless of contract terms, automatic **compound interest at 3× the RBI bank rate**, and enforcement by the state **Micro & Small Enterprises Facilitation Council** via **[samadhaan.msme.gov.in](https://samadhaan.msme.gov.in)** — file online with invoices and delivery proof. Councils conciliate then arbitrate; buyer appeals require **75% pre-deposit** of the award, which is why buyers settle. Not registered yet? Udyam registration is free and takes minutes — do it before the next invoice.

en.title: Court: summary suit — or insolvency pressure
en.summary: Order 37 for clean claims; IBC only above ₹1 crore.
en.detail: With written contracts and invoices, a **summary suit (Order 37 CPC)** denies the buyer a free defence — they need the court's leave. Claim interest and costs. **Lok Adalat** (see our [settlement guide](/help/money-recovery/friend-not-returning-money)) is the free compromise lane at any stage. The IBC operational-creditor route applies only to defaults of **₹1 crore+** — below that, it is notice + Samadhaan + suit. Cheque given and bounced? Run the [Section 138 route](/help/money-recovery/cheque-bounce-what-to-do) in parallel.

rows.label: Contract hardening for the future
rows.note: Vendor/service agreements with interest + jurisdiction clauses

en.footnote: Keep supplying only against cleared dues once an account turns sticky — fresh credit to a defaulting buyer is how recoverable claims become write-offs.

en.q: Does the 45-day MSME rule apply if my contract says 90-day payment terms?
en.a: Yes — the MSMED Act overrides longer contractual credit periods: the statutory maximum is 45 days from acceptance (15 days where no period is agreed). Interest at 3× bank rate compounds monthly from day 46 regardless of what the PO says. The registration date matters: supplies made after Udyam registration get the protection.

en.q: The buyer disputes quality to avoid paying. Does that kill the claim?
en.a: It complicates but rarely kills: contemporaneous delivery acceptance, absence of timely written rejection, continued use of the goods/work, and GST credit claimed on your invoice all cut against late-invented quality disputes. Councils and courts see the pattern constantly — your delivery documentation decides it.

en.q: How long does MSME Samadhaan actually take?
en.a: The Act contemplates 90 days for council disposal; real timelines run 6–18 months by state. The pressure works earlier though — many buyers settle on receiving the council notice, because the 75% pre-deposit for appeal makes losing expensive. File and keep negotiating.

en.q: My buyer is a big company. Any extra levers?
en.a: Several: listed and large companies must disclose MSME dues (MSME Form 1 half-yearly; audit disclosure of delayed payments), so a documented Samadhaan claim creates compliance noise. Interest to MSMEs is also income-tax disallowed for the buyer. Procurement teams settle to keep vendor-compliance records clean.

en.q: Can I recover the interest and legal costs too?
en.a: Yes — contractual/late-payment interest or MSMED statutory interest is claimable as of right, and courts/councils award costs. In summary suits, interest from due date to realisation is standard. Claim them explicitly in the notice so settlement negotiations start from the full number.

reviewer: Adv. Rohan Mehta, Bar Council of Maharashtra & Goa, MAH/2214/2011
author: WakilBhai Editorial Team
